description: Post-vaccine DTaP — matches mock data perfectly
question: My 4-month-old daughter Mia got her DTaP vaccine 2 days ago and has been sleeping more and eating less since then. Is this normal? Should I be worried?
reference_answer: Post-vaccination behavioral changes such as reduced feeding and increased sleepiness are normal and expected within 48-72 hours of DTaP vaccination. These are signs of a healthy immune response. Continue offering regular feedings, allow extra rest, and monitor for fever. Seek immediate care if fever exceeds 101°F for more than 48 hours, inconsolable crying lasts more than 3 hours, or the baby refuses to eat for more than 24 hours.

description: Feeding regression without vaccine context — stubs will still return vaccine advice
question: My 6-month-old son Leo has been refusing the bottle for the past 3 days. He used to drink 150ml per feed but now barely takes 80ml. Could this be a growth spurt?
reference_answer: Temporary feeding regressions around 6 months are common and can be related to growth spurts, teething, or developmental milestones. Monitor wet diapers to ensure adequate hydration (6+ per day). If refusal persists beyond 5 days or is accompanied by weight loss, consult your pediatrician.

description: High-risk fever + lethargy scenario — stubs always return LOW risk
question: My 3-month-old has a temperature of 102°F, is very lethargic, and has not eaten in 8 hours. What should I do?
reference_answer: A fever of 102°F in a 3-month-old combined with lethargy and feeding refusal requires immediate medical attention. Contact your pediatrician immediately or go to the emergency room. Do not wait. For infants under 3 months, any fever above 100.4°F is considered a medical emergency.

description: Routine milestone question — topic mismatch with stubs
question: When should my 5-month-old start sitting up on her own? She can hold her head steady but tips over when I prop her up.
reference_answer: Most babies begin sitting independently between 5-7 months. At 5 months, tipping over is completely normal. Encourage development with supervised tummy time and supported sitting practice. Each baby develops at their own pace.

description: Conflicting advice scenario — stubs have no conflict resolution logic
question: My pediatrician said to start solids at 4 months, but everything I read online says to wait until 6 months. My 4.5-month-old seems interested in food. What should I do?
reference_answer: Both AAP and WHO have nuanced positions on solid food introduction. AAP supports introduction around 4-6 months when readiness signs are present, while WHO recommends exclusive breastfeeding until 6 months. Readiness signs include head control, sitting with support, and showing interest in food. Discuss your specific baby's readiness with your pediatrician.
